elements.sent-2411-001: Reconfigurable electromagnetic structures (REMSs), such as reconfigurable reflectarrays (RRAs) or reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs), hold significant potential to improve wireless communication and sensing systems.
elements.sent-2411-002: Even though several REMS modeling approaches have been proposed in recent years, the literature lacks models that are both computationally efficient and physically consistent.
elements.sent-2411-003: As a result, algorithms that control the reconfigurable elements of REMSs (e.g., the phase shifts of an RIS) are often built on simplistic models that are inaccurate.
elements.sent-2411-004: To enable physically accurate REMS-parameter tuning, we present a new framework for efficient and physically consistent modeling of general REMSs.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our modeling method combines a circuit-theoretic approach with a new formalism that describes a REMS's interaction with the electromagnetic (EM) waves in its far-field region.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our modeling method enables efficient computation of the entire far-field radiation pattern for arbitrary configurations of the REMS reconfigurable elements once a single full-wave EM simulation of the non-reconfigurable parts of the REMS has been performed.
elements.sent-2411-007: The predictions made by the proposed framework align with the physical laws of classical electrodynamics and model effects caused by inter-antenna coupling, non-reciprocal materials, polarization, ohmic losses, matching losses, influence of metallic housings, noise from low-noise amplifiers, and noise arising in or received by antennas.
elements.sent-2411-008: In order to validate the efficiency and accuracy of our modeling approach, we (i) compare our modeling method to EM simulations and (ii) conduct a case study involving a planar RRA that enables simultaneous multiuser beam- and null-forming using a new, computationally efficient, and physically accurate parameter tuning algorithm.

elements.sent-2411-011: RRAs comprise one or more actively fed antennas and a nearby array of passive but reconfigurable reflecting elements.
elements.sent-2411-012: The reconfigurability of these reflective elements enables dynamic control over the antenna's behavior, mainly the RRA's beampattern, in order to accomplish several goals, such as maximizing gain, reducing side lobes, or minimizing cross-polarization.
elements.sent-2411-013: RALs consist of arrays of reconfigurable passive elements that function similarly to optical lenses, manipulating electromagnetic (EM) waves passing through the structure to achieve similar goals as RRAs.
elements.sent-2411-014: RISs, which have gained overwhelming popularity in recent years, typically correspond to planar EM structures with reconfigurable elements and are capable of dynamically manipulating their reflecting behavior.
elements.sent-2411-015: Typically, RISs are positioned between transmitter(s) and receiver(s) to dynamically improve the wireless channel for communication or the accuracy of sensing tasks.
elements.sent-2411-016: The popularity of REMS-related research has led to a significant rise in interest in REMS modeling in an efficient and physically consistent manner.
elements.sent-2411-017: In what follows, with efficient we refer to models (i) whose parameters can be obtained with acceptable computational effort at design time and (ii) that enable real-time prediction of the REMS behavior at manageable complexity; with physically consistent we refer to the alignment of the model's predictions with the physical laws of classical electrodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-018: This includes effects caused by inter-antenna coupling, non-reciprocal materials, polarization, ohmic losses, matching losses, influence of metallic housings, noise from low-noise amplifiers, and noise arising in or received by antennas.
elements.sent-2411-019: While full-wave EM simulations\footnote{Full-wave EM simulations numerically solve all of Maxwell's equations without any simplifying assumptions.}
elements.sent-2411-020: An example of a full-wave EM simulator is Ansys HFSS (short for High-Frequency Structure Simulator)~\cite{ansys_hffs .}
elements.sent-2411-021: For example, high complexity is an issue when modeling RISs, which must be quite large to have a noticeable impact on the propagation conditions.
elements.sent-2411-022: Simulating such large REMSs with conventional full-wave EM simulations demands extensive computational resources (memory as well as CPU time) and is prone to numerical stability issues.
elements.sent-2411-023: Consequently, modeling methods that rely on numerous full-wave EM simulations (e.g., for every possible parameter configuration of an RIS) cannot be considered efficient in the above-mentioned sense.
elements.sent-2411-024: Even though a wide variety of efficient REMS modeling approaches has been proposed in recent years, the literature lacks REMS models that are both efficient and physically consistent.
elements.sent-2411-025: In this paper, we fill this gap by proposing an efficient and physically consistent model for general REMS.\footnote{Conventional single- and multi-antenna transceivers and other non-reconfigurable EM systems are special cases of REMS and can therefore also be modeled with our framework.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Our main contributions are as follows.
elements.sent-2411-027: We propose a new, mathematically rigorous formalism to model an arbitrary REMS's interaction with the (incoming and outgoing) EM waves in its far-field region.
elements.sent-2411-028: We propose an efficient and physically-consistent REMS model by incorporating recent findings on circuit-theoretic models with our REMS-far-field-interaction formalism.
elements.sent-2411-029: We show how the model parameters required by our REMS-far-field-interaction formalism can be extracted from a single full-wave EM simulation.
elements.sent-2411-030: We validate the proposed REMS-far-field-interaction formalism and the method for obtaining the required model parameters in two scenarios by comparing them with Ansys HFSS simulations.
elements.sent-2411-031: We demonstrate the efficiency and accuracy of our REMS modeling approach with a case study of a planar RRA with two actively fed antennas that enable simultaneous multiuser beam- and null-forming using a computationally efficient and physics-aware algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-032: Finally, we discuss the limitations of our framework and outline potential future avenues.
elements.sent-2411-033: Various REMS models have been proposed in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-034: These models, which are often heuristically motivated, are typically highly efficient but lack either physical consistency or generality; that is, they are only physically consistent for certain specific scenarios.

elements.subsection-2411-010: Reciprocity Relations for the Radiating Structure
elements.sent-2411-209: In both purely academic and practical settings, it is often assumed that the wireless channel, including the radiating structure of all participating components, behaves in reciprocal manner.
elements.sent-2411-210: Whether a passive linear time-invariant wireless system behaves reciprocally or not is determined by the permittivity and permeability tensors describing the macroscopic material characteristics of the objects within the system.
elements.sent-2411-211: A sufficient condition for reciprocity is that all relevant materials are isotropic, meaning their properties do not change when tested in different directions.
elements.sent-2411-212: In this case, the permittivity and permeability tensors reduce to (position-dependent) scalar quantities.
elements.sent-2411-213: As a consequence of this, Lorenz reciprocity (cf.~\cite[Eq.~3.61]{balanis_antenna_theory ) holds.}
elements.sent-2411-214: i.e., in a source-free reciprocal system, it holds that \begin{equation}\oiint_{\partial V} \hat{\vect{n}}^\T \big( \phv{E}_1 \times \phv{H}_2 - \phv{E}_2 \times \phv{H}_1 \big) \up{d} S =\SI{0}{\watt}. \label{eq:lorenz_reciprocity_definition}\end{equation} Here, $V$ denotes an arbitrary volume within the source-free reciprocal system, $\up{d S$ denotes the area of the differential element on the surface of the volume $V$, $\phv{E _\ell$ and $\phv{H _\ell$ are the electric field and the magnetic field strength phasors, respectively, induced by one of two sources indexed by $\ell\in\{1,2\}$, and $\hat{\vect{n }$ is the outward pointing unit vector perpendicular to the surface $\partial V$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-215: In the following proposition, the impact of Lorentz reciprocity on the parameters of the radiation structure is explained.
elements.sent-2411-216: A proof of~\fref{prop:reciprocity can be found in~\fref{app:proof_reciprocity .}}

elements.section-2411-004: Case Study: Physically-Accurate Beam- and Nullforming with a Reconfigurable Reflectarray
elements.sent-2411-305: In order to demonstrate the efficiency and the physical consistency of the model proposed in~\fref{sec:modeling , we now provide a case study that focuses on a reconfigurable reflectarray (RRA) as depicted in~\fref{fig:case_study_RRA .\footnote{An Ansys HFSS project file containing the modeled RRA can be downloaded from \url{https://github.com/IIP-Group/RRA-case-study .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-306: This RRA is a hybrid analog-digital beamforming system, and we analyze its behavior when it acts as a transmitter.
elements.sent-2411-307: The goal of the RRA is to steer its transmit beams towards $U\leq N$ primary users (e.g., communication partners) while minimizing the power directed towards $I\in\mathbb{Z _{\geq 0}$ secondary users (e.g., other receiving devices that are not part of the communication system).}
elements.sent-2411-308: This RRA uses the basic structure illustrated in~\fref{fig:basic_structure_RRA , with $N=2$ actively fed patch antennas and a large number ($R=100$) of passive and configurable reflective elements arranged as a $10\times10$ rectangular array.}
elements.sent-2411-309: For $r\in [R]$, the $r$th passive reflective element features a tunable impedance~$Z_{\up{R ,r}$.}
elements.sent-2411-310: To perform simultaneous beam- and null-forming, we propose an efficient and physics-aware algorithm that jointly optimizes the configuration of the RRA's reconfigurable elements and the digital precoding matrix.
elements.sent-2411-311: We represent the values of the $R$ impedances in the tuning network with the tuple $\mat{Z _\up{R}\triangleq(Z_{\up{R _1 ,\ldots,Z_{\up{R _R )$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-312: Furthermore, we denote the finite set of potential impedance values that can be chosen for each reconfigurable impedance as $\mathcal{Z _\up{R}\subset \big\{c\in\mathbb{C}\big|\Re\{c\}\geq 0\big\}$\footnote{The impedance values of the reconfigurable elements are restricted to have a non-negative real part because the tuning network is assumed to be passive.}}
elements.sent-2411-313: For each primary user indexed by $u\in[U]$, let $s_u\in\mathbb{C}$ be the symbol to be transmitted to primary user $u$, and define the symbol vector as $\vect{s}\triangleq [s_1,\ldots,s_U]^\T$.
elements.sent-2411-314: Let~$\mat{T}\in\mathbb{C ^{N\times U}$ denote the precoding matrix, such that~$\phv{v _\up{Tx}\triangleq \mat{T}\vect{s}$ is the precoded PA output voltage vector.}}
elements.sent-2411-315: To steer the RRA's beams towards the primary users and minimize the energy towards the secondary users, the algorithm jointly optimizes the analog reconfigurable impedances $\mat{Z _\up{R}$ (equal to analog beam- and null-forming) and the precoding matrix $\mat{T}$ (equal to digital beam- and null-forming).}

elements.sent-2411-368: While the proposed modeling method is both efficient and physically consistent, it has the following key limitations.
elements.sent-2411-369: First,~\fref{asm:basic_assumption_radiating_part limits our model to REMSs with no movable parts.}
elements.sent-2411-370: This prevents our model from describing systems as the ones described in~\cite{lor_phon_lim_reconfigurable_transmissive_metasurface_with_a_combination_of_scissor_and_rotation_actuators_for_independently_controlling_beam_scanning_and_polarization_conversion,veljovic_skrivervik_ultralow_profile_circuilarly_polarized_reflectarray_antenna_for_cubesat_intersatellite_links_in_k_band,huang_pogorzelski_a_ka_band_microstrip_reflectarray_with_elements_having_variable_rotation_angles or so-called fluid antennas~\cite{wong_shojaeifard_tong_zhang_fluid_antenna_systems .}}
elements.sent-2411-371: However, as movable parts tend to have a high propensity for mechanical degradation or failures, it remains an open question what role such antenna systems will play in future wireless networks.
elements.sent-2411-372: Second, as discussed in~\fref{rem:sampling , we assumed that radiating structure kernels can be approximated arbitrarily well with sampled kernels.}
elements.sent-2411-373: However, we have neither discussed the number of required samples nor how an optimal sampling strategy would look like.
elements.sent-2411-374: A detailed investigation of both of these aspects, which relate to the mathematical complexity of a REMS's far field, is part of ongoing research.
elements.sent-2411-375: Third, our proposed model in~\fref{sec:modeling is designed for a narrowband analysis of REMSs.}
elements.sent-2411-376: However, our model can straightforwardly be generalized to enable a broadband analysis, for instance, by treating each frequency of interest separately.
elements.sent-2411-377: Nonetheless, it is an open question whether the model parameters for all frequencies of interest must be stored individually or if more memory- and compute-efficient approaches exist.
elements.sent-2411-378: In this work, we have proposed an efficient and physically-consistent modeling framework for reconfigurable electromagnetic structures (REMSs).
elements.sent-2411-379: Our rigorous mathematical formalism combines circuit-theoretic models with our REMS-to-far-field-interaction approach, which enables us to model the interaction of arbitrary REMSs with (incoming and outgoing) electromagnetic waves in the far-field region using a single full-wave electromagnetic (EM) simulation.
elements.sent-2411-380: We have validated our framework in two scenarios by comparing it to Ansys HFSS simulations, which reveals the efficiency and physical accuracy of our model.
elements.sent-2411-381: In addition, we have demonstrated the effectiveness of our model via a case study of a reconfigurable reflectarray (RRA) that performs joint multiuser beam- and null-forming using a novel computationally-efficient algorithm that simultaneously optimizes the digital beamforming matrix and the RRA's tunable parameters.
elements.sent-2411-382: All of these results confirm both the efficiency and physical consistency of our framework.
elements.sent-2411-383: There are many avenues for future work, some of which we outline next.
elements.sent-2411-384: First, systematically investigating how the kernels of the radiating structure should be sampled is an ongoing effort.
elements.sent-2411-385: Second, while our REMS model can be generalized to performing a wideband analysis, it is currently unclear whether the model parameters for all frequencies of interest must be stored or if more efficient representations exist.
elements.sent-2411-386: Third, the algorithm proposed in~\fref{sec:algorithm was heuristically motivated.}
elements.sent-2411-387: We believe that many refinements to our algorithm are possible, including alternative objective functions and more efficient parameter search strategies.

elements.sent-2411-603: We now expand each element~$\vect{g _n$ into the standard basis of~$\mathbb{R ^M$, denoted by~$\{\vect{e _{m'}\}_{m'\in[M]}$, and write \begin{equation}\big[\oper{S}_{\up{R}_{\up{R}\up{F}}}\vect{f}\big]_m = \Big[\sum_{n\in[M]} \alpha_n \langle \vect{f} , \tilde{\vect{g}}_n \rangle_{L^2} \vect{g}_n\Big]_m \nonumber = \Big[\sum_{n\in[M]} \alpha_n \langle \vect{f} , \tilde{\vect{g}}_n \rangle_{L^2} \sum_{m'\in[M]} \langle \vect{g}_n , \vect{e}_{m'}\rangle_{C^M} \vect{e}_{m'} \Big]_m \label{eq:proof_basic_form_SFTF_expansion_step} = \sum_{n\in[M]} \alpha_n \langle \vect{f} , \tilde{\vect{g}}_n \rangle_{L^2} [\vect{g}_n]_m \label{eq:proof_basic_form_SFTF_filter_step} = \big\langle \vect{f} , \underbrace{ \sum_{n\in[M]} \overline{(\alpha_n [\vect{g}_n]_m)} \tilde{\vect{g}}_n }_{\triangleq\,\overline{\vect{s}}_{\up{R}_{\up{R}\up{F}}}(m;\theta,\varphi)} \big\rangle_{L^2}, \label{eq:proof_basic_form_SFTF_linearity_step}\end{equation} where we perform the above-mentioned extension to obtain \eqref{eq:proof_basic_form_SFTF_expansion_step ; where \eqref{eq:proof_basic_form_SFTF_filter_step follows from the filter characteristics of the standard basis; and where \eqref{eq:proof_basic_form_SFTF_linearity_step is a consequence of the linearity characteristics of inner products.. \end{proofw.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-604: We will now demonstrate that the term~$P_\up{T}$ is upper bounded by \begin{equation}\label{eq:proof_PA:bound_1} P_\up{T} = \sum_{n\in [N]} \Re \big\{ \phv{v}_{\up{T},n}\phv{i}_{\up{T},n} \big\} = \sum_{n\in [N_\up{Tx}]} \Re \big\{ \phv{v}_{\up{T},n}\phv{i}_{\up{T},n} \big\} = \sum_{n\in [N_\up{Tx}]} \frac{1}{4 \Re\{\mat{Z}_{\up{Tx},n}\}} \Big( \left| \phv{v}_{\up{T},n} + \mat{Z}_{\up{Tx},n} \phv{i}_{\up{T},n} \right|^2 \nonumber \;\;\; - \left| \phv{v}_{\up{T},n} - \mat{Z}_{\up{Tx},n} \phv{i}_{\up{T},n} \right|^2 \Big) \label{eq:proof_PA_leq} \leq \sum_{n\in [N_\up{Tx}]} \frac{1}{4 \Re\{\mat{Z}_{\up{Tx},n}\}} \left| \phv{v}_{\up{T},n} + \mat{Z}_{\up{Tx},n} \phv{i}_{\up{T},n} \right|^2 = \sum_{n\in [N_\up{Tx}]} \frac{1}{4} \overline{ \left( \phv{v}_{\up{T},n} + \mat{Z}_{\up{Tx},n} \phv{i}_{\up{T},n} \right) } \Re\{\mat{Z}_{\up{Tx},n}\}^{-1} \left( \phv{v}_{\up{T},n} + \mat{Z}_{\up{Tx},n} \phv{i}_{\up{T},n} \right) = \sum_{n\in [N_\up{Tx}]} \frac{1}{4} \overline{ \phv{v} }_{\up{Tx},n} \Re\{\mat{Z}_{\up{Tx},n}\}^{-1} \phv{v}_{\up{Tx},n} = \label{eq:proof_PA:bound} \frac{1}{4} \phv{v}_\up{Tx}^H \Re\{\mat{Z}_\up{Tx}\}^{-1} \phv{v}_\up{Tx}.\end{equation}.
